stocks.note: Acquired by Microsoft for $95.00/share all-cash, closed Oct 13, 2023; delisted from Nasdaq. Anchors are split-adjusted (3:2 split 2008). Stock ran from ~$65 to low-$80s on the Jan 2022 announcement, then drifted ~$75-80 through 2022-2023 on regulatory uncertainty before closing near the $95 deal price. Pre-2008 figures reflect the legacy Activision, Inc. (merged with Vivendi's Blizzard in July 2008).

Acquired by Oracle for $95.00/share all-cash; tender offer completed June 8, 2022, delisted from Nasdaq. Final trades hugged the $95 deal price (~$94.92). Anchors split-adjusted (Cerner did several 2:1 splits in the 2000s). Long-term uptrend in healthcare IT from high-single-digits in early 2000s to low-$90s at acquisition; no order-of-magnitude jumps.

Acquired by Vista Equity Partners and Evergreen Coast Capital (Elliott affiliate) for $104.00/share all-cash, deal closed Sept 30, 2022; merged with TIBCO to form Cloud Software Group, delisted from Nasdaq. Acquirer is private (no public ticker). All-time intraday/EOD high ~$167.73 on July 20, 2020; finished 2020 near $130, then traded toward the $104 deal price. Anchors split-adjusted; consistent basis.

Equity wiped out in the March-May 2023 regional banking crisis. Re-IPO'd as a standalone bank in Dec 2010 at ~$25-29. Steady climb to a Nov 2021 peak over $200 (intraday ~$220); ended 2022 near $122. Collapsed in spring 2023, closing $3.51 on Apr 28, 2023; FDIC seized the bank May 1, 2023 and sold assets to JPMorgan Chase, leaving common holders ~$0. Anchors on consistent unadjusted basis (no splits); steep but real drop to near zero at the end.

Acquired by a private-equity consortium led by Evergreen Coast Capital (Elliott) and Brookfield Business Partners for $28.00/share all-cash; deal completed Oct 11, 2022, delisted from NYSE (last trade Oct 11, 2022). Acquirers are private (no public ticker). IPO'd late Dec 2010 at ~$23-25; peaked ~$55.81 in July 2016, then declined into the low-$20s by 2018-2021 before the buyout lifted it to ~$28. Consistent basis, no splits.

Equity wiped out in the March 2023 collapse of Silicon Valley Bank. Stock peaked at ~$755 intraday in early Nov 2021 (year-end 2021 ~$679). Fell ~60% on Mar 9, 2023; trading halted Mar 10, 2023 with a last print of $106.04 just before the FDIC seized the bank. SVB Financial parent filed Chapter 11; common equity went to ~$0. Anchors on consistent unadjusted basis; large but real run-up and crash, no spurious magnitude jumps.

Twitter IPO'd Nov 2013 at $26 (opened ~$45). Acquired by Elon Musk-controlled X Holdings for $54.20/share cash; deal closed Oct 27, 2022 and stock was delisted. Acquirer is private (X Corp), no public acquirer symbol. Prices on consistent split-free basis (no splits).

Semiconductor (FPGA) maker. Acquired by AMD in an all-stock deal; each XLNX share converted to 1.7234 AMD shares. Deal closed Feb 14, 2022; XLNX delisted. Prices reflect normal large-cap semiconductor range, single split-adjusted basis.

Oil & gas E&P. Acquired by Occidental Petroleum; each APC share received $59.00 cash plus 0.2934 OXY shares (implied value ~$72-73 at announcement). Deal closed Aug 8, 2019; APC delisted. Mixed cash-and-stock consideration. Prices split-adjusted (2-for-1 split in 2008 accounted for) on consistent basis.

Enterprise communications company spun off from Lucent in Oct 2000, traded NYSE as AV. Acquired by private equity firms Silver Lake and TPG Capital for $17.50/share cash; deal closed Oct 26, 2007 and stock was delisted (taken private). No public acquirer symbol. Note: AV ticker later reused by other entities; this is the original Avaya.

Identified as Federated Department Stores (operator of Macy's and Bloomingdale's). Federated traded NYSE primarily as 'FD'; the 'FDXF' symbol with an F suffix is likely a data-source artifact for the same security. Federated acquired May Department Stores in 2005, then renamed itself Macy's, Inc. and changed ticker to 'M' on June 1, 2007 (1:1 continuation, not an acquisition/wipeout). Prices reflect normal retail large-cap range (pre-2005 May merger ~$30-50, peak ~$70s in 2005). Low confidence on exact symbol mapping; magnitudes are realistic for Federated/Macy's.

Credit-card issuer (subprime). Stock peaked ~$60s in 2000-early 2001 then collapsed in late 2001 (from ~$50 to under $4) on credit-quality crisis. Recovered over 2002-2005. Acquired by Washington Mutual (ticker WM); each PVN share received 0.4005 WaMu shares plus $2.00 cash. Deal closed Oct 3, 2005; PVN delisted. Implied deal value ~$18.71/share. Mixed cash-and-stock consideration. The sharp 2001 drop is a real historical event, not a basis inconsistency.

IMS Health (NYSE: RX), pharmaceutical market-intelligence firm. Taken private Feb 2010 by TPG Capital, CPP Investment Board and Leonard Green & Partners at $22.00/share cash. (Successor IQVIA is unrelated to this ticker.) Year-end closes from 1stock1; deal price confirmed. Ticker RX is now reused by an unrelated security.

Schering-Plough (NYSE: SGP), major pharma. Merged into Merck Nov 3 2009; each SGP share received 0.5767 MRK shares + $10.50 cash (~$28.19 value at close). Stock fell sharply 2002-04 on Claritin patent loss and accounting issues, then recovered. Year-end levels approximate from knowledge; merger terms confirmed.

Rockwell Collins (NYSE: COL), avionics maker spun off from Rockwell International on June 29 2001 (1 COL per Rockwell share). Acquired by United Technologies (UTX) Nov 26 2018 at $140/share (cash + UTX stock); final trades ~$141 confirmed. Mid-decade year-end levels approximate from knowledge; spin and deal terms confirmed.

Merrill Lynch (NYSE: MER), brokerage/investment bank. Acquired by Bank of America effective Jan 1 2009 at 0.8595 BAC shares per MER share. Peaked ~$95-98 early 2007, collapsed in the 2008 financial crisis; last MER close ~$13-19 end of Dec 2008. Year-end levels approximate from knowledge; merger ratio confirmed.

Great Lakes Chemical (NYSE: GLK), specialty-chemicals maker. All-stock merger with Crompton Corp (NYSE: CK) closed July 1 2005, forming Chemtura (later NYSE: CEM); each GLK share converted to 2.2232 CK shares. Traded roughly mid-$20s to low-$30s in early 2000s. Year-end levels approximate from knowledge; merger ratio and date confirmed.

Kmart Corporation (NYSE: KM), discount retailer. Filed Chapter 11 on Jan 22 2002 (then-largest US retail bankruptcy); shares fell from a ~$13.55 Aug 2001 high to under $1. NYSE delisted KM in Dec 2002 (moved to Pink Sheets as KMRTQ) and the old common equity was wiped out in the 2003 reorganization. New post-bankruptcy Kmart Holding stock (later merged with Sears) is a separate security. Year-end/event levels approximate; bankruptcy and delisting confirmed.

Spanish-language broadcaster (NYSE: UVN). Taken private March 29, 2007 by Broadcasting Media Partners (Madison Dearborn, Providence Equity, TPG, Thomas H. Lee, Saban) at $36.25/share cash; deal announced June 2006. Traded ~$25-37 over 2001-2007. Acquirer is a private consortium, so no acquirer ticker. Today's UVN ticker is reused by an unrelated security; ignore modern data.

NY-based commercial/crypto-focused bank (Nasdaq: SBNY). IPO March 2004 ~$15. Climbed to all-time high ~$375 in early 2022 (year-end 2021 ~$323). Closed by NY DFS and placed into FDIC receivership March 12, 2023 amid the regional-bank crisis; common equity wiped out, shares moved to OTC near zero. Operating assets/deposits sold to NYCB (Flagstar), but SBNY shareholders received nothing.

Commercial lender CIT Group (NYSE: CIT), re-IPO'd July 2002 after spin-off from Tyco. Traded ~$35-55 in 2003-2006, collapsed in the 2008 financial crisis. Filed Chapter 11 Nov 1, 2009; delisted to OTC as CITGQ. Plan confirmed and emerged Dec 10, 2009 — old common stock cancelled (wiped out); creditors received new equity in the reorganized company, which relisted under CIT. The CITGQ ('Q' = bankruptcy) ticker tracked the doomed old equity only.

Independent power producer Calpine (NYSE: CPN). Peaked near $58 in 2001, then crashed as wholesale power prices collapsed post-Enron. Filed Chapter 11 Dec 20, 2005 (delisted to OTC CPNLQ, trading in pennies). Emerged from bankruptcy Jan/Feb 2008 — old CPNLQ common cancelled (wiped out); reorganized Calpine issued new shares and relisted under CPN. Note: a new, unrelated Calpine equity later traded under CPN until taken private by Energy Capital Partners in 2018; that is NOT the CPNLQ security.

Pharmacy benefit manager Caremark Rx (NYSE: CMX). Merged into CVS to form CVS Caremark, closed March 22, 2007. Caremark holders received 1.67 CVS shares per Caremark share plus a $7.50/share special cash dividend (cash_per_share reflects this special dividend; the main consideration was stock). Split-adjusted basis is consistent across anchors (Caremark did a 2:1 split in 2004, reflected here). Modern CMX ticker is a reuse by an unrelated security.

Pharmacy benefit manager Medco Health Solutions (NYSE: MHS), spun off from Merck Aug 2003. Acquired by Express Scripts (ESRX), closed April 2, 2012: Medco holders received $28.80 cash + 0.81 ESRX shares per share, ~$71.36 total. All anchors are on a consistent split-adjusted basis (2-for-1 split distributed Jan 2008); pre-split late-2007 ~$100/share equals ~$50 split-adjusted. Modern MHS ticker is reused by an unrelated security.

SunGard Data Systems (NYSE: SDS), financial-software and disaster-recovery firm. Taken private Aug 2005 in an $11.4B LBO by a consortium led by Silver Lake (incl. Bain, Blackstone, GS Capital, KKR, Providence, TPG) at $36.00/share cash; delisted. Anchors are on the post-May-2002 3-for-2 split basis (the $58.75 close of 3/6/2001 equals ~$39 post-split). Acquirer was a private-equity group, so no public acquirer ticker. Mid-$20s to ~$36 trading range; no order-of-magnitude jumps.

Titanium Metals Corp (TIMET), NYSE: TIE. Acquired by Precision Castparts (PCP) in Dec 2012 for $16.50/share cash. CRITICAL BASIS NOTE: anchors are reconstructed on ONE consistent final/modern split-adjusted basis ending at the $16.50 acquisition price. TIE was genuinely a sub-$1 penny stock in 2001-2003 (the 2002-2003 titanium-industry trough), then rallied ~1000%+ in 2004-2005 on aerospace demand (a real price move, NOT a split artifact). The often-quoted 2004-2006 closes near $120 from older return tables are on a stale 1996-era split basis and do NOT match the $16.50 exit; those have been re-based here (divided ~7.15x) so all anchors are internally consistent. The 2007-2012 figures (26.45, 8.81, 12.52, 17.18, 14.98, 16.50) are already on the final basis. Confidence medium due to the multiple reverse/forward splits (1-for-10 reverse 2003, 5-for-1 2004, 2-for-1 2005).

Thomas & Betts (NYSE: TNB), electrical-components maker. Acquired by ABB Ltd. (NYSE: ABB) in 2012 for $72.00/share cash (deal announced Jan 2012, completed mid-2012); delisted. Clean year-end closing series on a single consistent split-adjusted basis (~$17-55 trading range, ending at the $72 cash price). No order-of-magnitude jumps.

Union Planters Corp (NYSE: UPC), Memphis-based bank holding company. Merged with Regions Financial in a 'merger of equals' completed July 1, 2004; each UPC share converted into 1.0 share of the new Regions Financial Corporation (NYSE: RF) — UPC shareholders received the new RF stock 1-for-1 (Regions holders got 1.2346). UPC ceased trading July 2004. Traded as a regional bank in the high-$20s to low-$30s; final value ~$32 reflecting RF's price at close. Consistent basis, no order-of-magnitude jumps.

Cinergy Corp (NYSE: CIN), Cincinnati-based electric & gas utility (Cincinnati Gas & Electric, PSI Energy). Merged into Duke Energy (NYSE: DUK), completed April 3, 2006; each Cinergy share converted into 1.56 shares of new Duke Energy common stock. CIN delisted April 2006. Traded as a dividend utility in the low-to-mid $30s (2001-2003) rising to ~$42 (2005); final value ~$49 (= 1.56 x Duke's ~$31 price at close). Jan 31 2003 last sale was $31.92 (per SEC filing). NOTE: Duke later did a 1-for-3 reverse split in 2012, which affects DUK basis but not these Cinergy-basis anchors. Consistent basis throughout.

Hercules Inc (NYSE: HPC), specialty-chemicals maker (paper/water-treatment chemicals). Acquired by Ashland Inc. (NYSE: ASH); deal announced July 11, 2008, completed Nov 2008. Mixed consideration: $18.60/share cash PLUS 0.093 Ashland share per HPC share (total deal value ~$23/share at announcement; the stock portion was worth ~$0.98 by 12/31/2008 after ASH fell). cash_per_share=18.60 and share_ratio=0.093 both apply. Clean ~$9-19 trading range on one consistent basis; final cash component $18.60. No order-of-magnitude jumps.

STILL ACTIVE on Nasdaq as NKTR. Clinical-stage biotech; was Inhale Therapeutic Systems until Jan 2003 rename to Nektar. All anchors are on the AS-TRADED (raw, pre-split) basis to keep one consistent basis with realistic tens-of-dollars magnitudes. Real history: ~$8-22 in early 2000s, Exubera collapse late 2007, then a massive immuno-oncology run peaking ~$108 on 12-Mar-2018 (BMS bempeg deal). Bempeg trial failures (Mar/Apr 2022) and lupus failure (Feb 2023) crushed it to a real all-time low of ~$0.41 as-traded (13-Nov-2023). A 1-for-15 REVERSE SPLIT took effect 8-Jun-2025; post-split it trades ~$61 (18-Jun-2026), which equals ~$4.07 on the pre-split as-traded basis used here. CAUTION: third-party data providers (macrotrends/yahoo) show the entire history multiplied 15x by the 2025 reverse split, so the 2018 peak appears as ~$1,670 and the 2023 low as ~$6.19 on their split-adjusted basis -- those are NOT corruption, just a different basis. Final anchor expressed pre-split-equivalent for consistency.

ORIGINAL SanDisk Corporation (flash memory; NASDAQ:SNDK 1995-2016), NOT the modern reuse. Acquired by Western Digital (WDC), closed 12-May-2016: each SNDK share received $67.50 CASH + 0.2387 WDC shares, total value ~$78.50/share (the originally-planned $85.10 cash + 0.0176 WDC structure was replaced after the Unis/CFIUS issue). Anchors are on the final split-adjusted basis (two 2-for-1 splits, Feb 2004 and 2006). Real arc: ~$10-45 mid-2000s, crashed to a ~$5.5 low Nov 2008 in the flash glut/financial crisis, recovered, peaked ~$108 in 2014, ~$76 at buyout. CRITICAL: the SNDK ticker was REUSED on 24-Feb-2025 by the new SanDisk spinoff from Western Digital, which trades ~$2,300 in 2026 -- that modern security is almost certainly the source of the order-of-magnitude corruption and must be ignored for the original company.
